note: Only HAR-002, HAR-004, HAR-013, HAR-019, HAR-020, HAR-024 are revised. All other HARs from Agent3_Output_apache_shiro.json remain unchanged.

revised_human_analysis_records.summary: JcaCipherService IV generation fully confirmed from revised CB-002 (rev2). generateInitializationVector() calls SecureRandom.nextBytes() on a 128-bit byte array. IV is prepended to ciphertext output (IV || ciphertext). generateInitializationVectors=true by default. SHA1PRNG SecureRandom algorithm with platform fallback. initializationVectorSize defaults to 128 bits matching keySize.
raw_observations: encrypt() prepend path: output = IV(16 bytes) || encrypted bytes
SHA1PRNG as default SecureRandom algorithm with platform fallback
IvParameterSpec created from IV bytes in initNewCipher()
uncertainties: SHA1PRNG is the named default but JDK may substitute platform PRNG on some environments — not a security risk but worth noting for reproducibility
follow_up_suggestions: No critical follow-up. IV mechanism fully confirmed. SHA1PRNG is acceptable for IV generation — not used for key derivation.
revised_human_analysis_records.notes_for_planner: FU-001 resolved. IV generation confirmed: cryptographically random (SecureRandom), 128-bit, prepended to ciphertext. No static or derived IV — strong implementation.

reasoning_trace.finding: SecureRandom.nextBytes(ivBytes) fills 128-bit IV array
reasoning_trace.interpretation: IV is cryptographically random — not static or derived

reasoning_trace.finding: Defaults to DEFAULT_KEY_SIZE=128 bits in JcaCipherService constructor
reasoning_trace.interpretation: 128-bit IV — appropriate for AES-GCM

reasoning_trace.finding: output = System.arraycopy(iv) + System.arraycopy(encrypted) — IV prepended to ciphertext
reasoning_trace.interpretation: IV prepend-to-ciphertext confirmed from method body — not from Javadoc only

reasoning_trace.finding: SHA1PRNG with fallback to new SecureRandom()
reasoning_trace.interpretation: SecureRandom algorithm identified — SHA1PRNG acceptable for IV generation

reasoning_trace.finding: key_size=128, mode=GCM inherited from AesCipherService (AT-001, HAR-001 confirmed) — cross-ref to confirmed crypto_finding
reasoning_trace.interpretation: No escalation violation — parent class parameters are directly confirmed

reasoning_trace.finding: Grover reduces to ~64-bit effective security — symmetric_security_margin_relevant
reasoning_trace.interpretation: AES-256 recommended for PQC long-term

reasoning_trace.finding: All mandatory full method body elements confirmed from revised CB-002 (rev2)
reasoning_trace.interpretation: outcome: crypto_finding, confidence: high

revised_human_analysis_records.summary: Argon2Hash default parameters fully confirmed from revised CB-004 (rev2): DEFAULT_ITERATIONS=1, DEFAULT_MEMORY_KIB=65536 (64 MiB), DEFAULT_PARALLELISM=4, DEFAULT_OUTPUT_LENGTH_BITS=256, SALT_LENGTH_BITS=128. Full generate() call chain confirmed. Parameters sourced from draft-irtf-cfrg-argon2-13 section 4.2. OWASP 2023 minimum for argon2id: m=19456, t=2, p=1 — Shiro's t=1 is below OWASP iteration recommendation but memory=64 MiB significantly exceeds minimum.
uncertainties: DEFAULT_ITERATIONS=1 is at OWASP 2023 minimum boundary (t=2 recommended) — memory=64 MiB partially compensates but strict OWASP compliance requires t>=2
follow_up_suggestions: Flag DEFAULT_ITERATIONS=1 for PQC/security assessment: OWASP 2023 recommends t=2 for argon2id. Consider whether Shiro should bump default to t=2 in a future release.
revised_human_analysis_records.notes_for_planner: FU-002 resolved. All Argon2 default parameters confirmed. security_concern kept as no_security_concern_identified: RFC-compliant defaults. OWASP t=1 boundary noted in uncertainties for human reviewer attention.

reasoning_trace.finding: DEFAULT_ITERATIONS=1 confirmed from source constant
reasoning_trace.interpretation: t=1 is RFC draft-13 section 4.2 value for memory-constrained env — at OWASP minimum boundary

reasoning_trace.finding: DEFAULT_MEMORY_KIB=64*1024=65536 confirmed
reasoning_trace.interpretation: 64 MiB memory — well above OWASP 19 MiB minimum

reasoning_trace.finding: p=4, output=256 bits, salt=128 bits all confirmed
reasoning_trace.interpretation: All security-critical parameters now directly observed

reasoning_trace.finding: Full generate() call chain confirmed — parameters passed correctly to BouncyCastle
reasoning_trace.interpretation: BouncyCastle Argon2 implementation wiring confirmed

reasoning_trace.finding: OWASP 2023 recommends t>=2 for argon2id — Shiro default is t=1
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Borderline compliance — kept as no_security_concern_identified since RFC-sourced, noted in uncertainties

reasoning_trace.finding: 256-bit hash output — Grover reduces to 128-bit effective; adequate for PQC
reasoning_trace.interpretation: hash_security_margin_relevant

reasoning_trace.finding: All parameters confirmed from revised CB-004 (rev2)
reasoning_trace.interpretation: outcome: crypto_finding, confidence: high

revised_human_analysis_records.summary: BlowfishCipherService fully confirmed from revised CB-018 (rev2): ALGORITHM_NAME='Blowfish', BLOCK_SIZE=64 bits, default mode CBC, PKCS5 padding, IV size 64 bits. Repository-wide caller search returned no production instantiation — class is public API dead code. 64-bit block size is the primary weakness (birthday attack at ~32 GB). Class exists in production src/ — must be documented in CBOM as available weak cipher.
raw_observations: No production caller found in grep sweep
uncertainties: DefaultBlockCipherService parent default mode/padding not directly read in this revision — CBC/PKCS5 values are from BlowfishCipherService Javadoc table, not parent source
follow_up_suggestions: Confirm DefaultBlockCipherService.setMode()/setPaddingScheme() defaults to verify CBC/PKCS5 are actually set by parent constructor.
Consider deprecating or removing BlowfishCipherService from public API given 64-bit block weakness.
revised_human_analysis_records.notes_for_planner: FU-003 resolved. Blowfish class confirmed with all parameters. No active caller — dead code. review_level L3: weak_algorithm + high confidence triggers mandatory human escalation in Agent 3.5.

reasoning_trace.finding: ALGORITHM_NAME='Blowfish', BLOCK_SIZE=64 bits confirmed from source constants
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Blowfish 64-bit block cipher confirmed

reasoning_trace.finding: CBC mode, PKCS5 padding, IV=64 bits confirmed from BlowfishCipherService Javadoc table
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Full cipher configuration confirmed

reasoning_trace.finding: 64-bit block: birthday attack probability ~50% after 2^32 blocks (~32 GB plaintext)
reasoning_trace.interpretation: security_concern: weak_algorithm — 64-bit block is fundamentally weak regardless of key size

reasoning_trace.finding: No production instantiation found in grep sweep
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Dead code in public API — risk is API exposure, not active exploitation

reasoning_trace.finding: 128-bit key, Grover reduces to 64-bit — symmetric_security_margin_relevant
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Block size weakness dominates over key size concern

reasoning_trace.finding: Class confirmed with all parameters; weak algorithm established from source
reasoning_trace.interpretation: outcome: crypto_finding, confidence: high

revised_human_analysis_records.summary: PaddingScheme enum fully confirmed from revised CB-024 (rev2). 11 values: NONE, ISO10126, OAEP, OAEPWithMd5AndMgf1, OAEPWithSha1AndMgf1, OAEPWithSha256AndMgf1, OAEPWithSha384AndMgf1, OAEPWithSha512AndMgf1, PKCS1, PKCS5, SSL3. Security concerns: PKCS1Padding (Bleichenbacher-vulnerable for RSA), OAEPWithMD5AndMGF1Padding (MD5 in OAEP), SSL3Padding (deprecated SSL context).

labels.raw_algorithm_text: PaddingScheme { NONE/NoPadding, PKCS1/PKCS1Padding, PKCS5/PKCS5Padding, OAEP variants, SSL3Padding }

raw_observations: 11 enum values confirmed: NONE, ISO10126, OAEP, OAEPWithMd5AndMgf1, OAEPWithSha1AndMgf1, OAEPWithSha256AndMgf1, OAEPWithSha384AndMgf1, OAEPWithSha512AndMgf1, PKCS1, PKCS5, SSL3
PKCS1Padding: vulnerable to Bleichenbacher attack (PKCS#1 v1.5 for RSA)
OAEPWithMD5AndMGF1Padding: MD5 used in OAEP digest — weak hash in asymmetric context
uncertainties: No active caller using PKCS1 or SSL3 padding found in grep hits — risk is API availability, not confirmed active use
follow_up_suggestions: Search codebase for setPaddingScheme(PaddingScheme.PKCS1) or SSL3 usage to confirm no active dangerous padding selection.
Consider deprecating PKCS1, SSL3, OAEPWithMd5AndMgf1 in a future Shiro release.
revised_human_analysis_records.notes_for_planner: FU-004 resolved. security_concern: insecure_padding assigned because PKCS1Padding is Bleichenbacher-vulnerable and present in the public API. padding field set to PKCS1Padding as the most critical insecure value.

reasoning_trace.finding: 11 padding values confirmed — PKCS1, SSL3, OAEPWithMd5AndMgf1 are security concerns
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Insecure padding options available in public API

reasoning_trace.finding: PKCS#1 v1.5 RSA padding is Bleichenbacher-vulnerable — oracle attack enabling private key recovery
reasoning_trace.interpretation: security_concern: insecure_padding — most critical value

reasoning_trace.finding: MD5 in OAEP digest — collision-vulnerable hash in asymmetric context
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Additional risk; lower severity than PKCS1

reasoning_trace.finding: SSL3Padding references deprecated SSL 3.0 specification
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Deprecated padding scheme — should not be used

reasoning_trace.finding: Padding schemes do not directly contribute to quantum vulnerability — cipher and key selection matters more
reasoning_trace.interpretation: not_quantum_relevant at padding layer

reasoning_trace.finding: Full enum confirmed with security concerns identified
reasoning_trace.interpretation: outcome: crypto_finding, confidence: high

revised_human_analysis_records.summary: JdbcRealm doGetAuthenticationInfo() fully confirmed from revised CB-025 (rev2). Default saltStyle=NO_SALT — returns raw DB password to CredentialsMatcher without salt. Hash algorithm delegated to externally configured CredentialsMatcher (not in JdbcRealm source). EXTERNAL saltStyle calls getSaltForUser() which returns the username — insecure predictable salt default. No hash operation in JdbcRealm itself.
raw_observations: saltStyle=SaltStyle.NO_SALT is the default field value
NO_SALT case: password = getPasswordForUser()[0] — raw DB string, no salt applied
EXTERNAL case: getSaltForUser() returns username — predictable salt
Hash algorithm not present in JdbcRealm — delegated to CredentialsMatcher
SimpleAuthenticationInfo stores raw password.toCharArray() — comparison by matcher
uncertainties: Actual hash algorithm used for credential comparison depends on CredentialsMatcher configuration — not determinable from JdbcRealm source alone
Whether deployers configure HashedCredentialsMatcher (correct) vs SimpleCredentialsMatcher (plaintext) is a deployment concern
follow_up_suggestions: Inspect default CredentialsMatcher wiring in AuthorizingRealm parent to determine if HashedCredentialsMatcher is the framework default.
Document getSaltForUser()=username insecure default in CBOM security_concern field.
revised_human_analysis_records.notes_for_planner: FU-005 resolved. security_concern: weak_algorithm assigned for getSaltForUser()=username (predictable salt). Hash algo delegation to CredentialsMatcher is a design pattern — not a bug — but requires correct deployer configuration. review_level L3: configuration_driven + security concern.

reasoning_trace.finding: saltStyle=SaltStyle.NO_SALT is the default — no salt applied to password by default
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Unsalted credential storage by default — security risk if CredentialsMatcher does not hash

reasoning_trace.finding: NO_SALT: password=getPasswordForUser()[0] passed directly to SimpleAuthenticationInfo
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Raw DB string passed to matcher — hash depends entirely on matcher configuration

reasoning_trace.finding: EXTERNAL saltStyle: getSaltForUser() returns username — attacker who knows username can precompute salt
reasoning_trace.interpretation: security_concern: weak_algorithm (predictable salt is equivalent to no salt against targeted attacks)

reasoning_trace.finding: Hash algorithm NOT in JdbcRealm source — delegated to CredentialsMatcher. Cannot confirm algorithm from this source.
reasoning_trace.interpretation: algorithm_name: unknown — escalation prohibited

reasoning_trace.finding: Credential mechanism confirmed; salt and hash algorithm risks identified
reasoning_trace.interpretation: outcome: crypto_finding, confidence: high

revised_human_analysis_records.summary: SaltAwareJdbcRealm fully confirmed from revised CB-029 (rev2). Salt = ByteSource.Util.bytes(username) — username used directly as salt. Source code comment explicitly warns 'DON'T DO THIS IN A REAL APP!' — demonstrates insecure salt pattern for educational purposes. Sample code under samples/spring-mvc. Hash algorithm still delegated to CredentialsMatcher.
raw_observations: Source comment: 'This (very bad) example uses the username as the salt... DON'T DO THIS IN A REAL APP!'
Extends JdbcRealm — inherits saltStyle and CredentialsMatcher delegation
uncertainties: Hash algorithm delegated to CredentialsMatcher — not in SaltAwareJdbcRealm source
Whether this sample is referenced by any production configuration in the test suite not confirmed
follow_up_suggestions: This record is a valuable negative training example: explicitly documented insecure salt pattern with source warning. Flag for golden dataset candidate as a security anti-pattern example.
revised_human_analysis_records.notes_for_planner: FU-006 resolved. Username-as-salt anti-pattern confirmed with explicit source code warning. Valuable training example — security anti-pattern explicitly documented in source. review_level L3: security concern + configuration_driven.

reasoning_trace.finding: Username string used as credential salt
reasoning_trace.interpretation: Predictable salt — attacker knows username, can precompute targeted rainbow table

reasoning_trace.finding: Source comment explicitly: 'DON'T DO THIS IN A REAL APP! Salts should not be based on anything that a user could enter'
